In brief
Kirschstein et al. discover that TGFβ
receptor signaling in myofibroblasts
blocks the differentiation of reticular
fibroblasts associated with TLS formation
in murine PDAC. Antagonizing TGFβR1
allows LTBR-dependent T and B cell
recruitment and increases tumor control.
This work demonstrates barriers to TLS
formation in PDAC applicable to other
immunotherapy-resistant solid tumors.
Highlights
LTBR induces early-TLS aggregates in some murine PDACs
TGFβ antagonizes TNFα/LTBR-mediated reticular fibroblast
programming
TGFβR inhibition and αLTBR promote reticular CAF and
T cell-mediated tumor control
Reticular CAF reside proximally to TLS in human PDAC

SUMMARY
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is strongly resistant to immunotherapy. However, recent evi-
dence shows that some PDAC tumors contain tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) associated with improved
survival. Why TLS forms in some tumors but not others remains elusive. Using a lymphotoxin beta receptor
(LTBR) agonist, we observe the induction of TLS-aggregates in some murine PDAC tumor models but not
others. The phenotypes of cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) in TLS-resistant models are myofibroblastic
(myCAF), whereas TLS-permissive models are enriched with reticular-CAF (rCAF) subsets. Differentiation
into myCAF blocks the LTBR-mediated upregulation of chemokines and lymphocyte migration toward fibro-
blasts. Inhibiting the transforming growth factor β (TGFβ) receptor, combined with LTBR agonism, promotes
TLS formation and T cell-dependent tumor control. In patient tumors, rCAF are proximal to TLS, while myCAF
are distally located. These data indicate that myCAF represses rCAF programming critical for TLS formation
but can be therapeutically remodeled to promote immune control of PDAC tumors.
INTRODUCTION
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is an aggressive and
highly lethal malignancy due to the lack of early detection and
therapeutic insensitivity.1 Nearly half of patients with PDAC are
diagnosed with metastatic disease, while also having a less
than
objective response rate to current immunotherapies.2–4
Thus, there is an urgent need to develop improved treatments
and novel biomarkers that will improve survival. Despite resis-
tance to immunotherapies, we and others have reported signifi-
cant lymphocyte infiltration in tumors from a subset of patients
with PDAC that is equivalent or even superior to other cancer
types, many of which do respond to immunotherapy.5–7 Further-
more, these lymphocyte-inflamed signatures associate with
longer overall survival.8 Hence, despite the belief that pancreatic
cancer is a poorly immunogenic tumor type, many patients with
PDAC mount nascent immune responses that conceivably can
be harnessed to control tumors. One unique lymphocyte-in-
flamed subtype, those with tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs),
is prognostic for survival in all solid tumor types, including
PDAC, and predictive of response to immune checkpoint
blockade in melanoma and sarcoma.9–13 TLS are a lymph
node-like aggregation comprised of T cells, B cells, and anti-
gen-presenting cells observed in chronically inflamed peripheral
tissues, such as solid tumors.13 Our prior study of treatment-
naı¨ve patients with PDAC showed that
of surgically re-
sected tumors displayed histological presence of TLS associ-
ated with improved survival and downregulated transforming
growth factor
\beta ( \mathsf { T G F } \beta )
signaling.7 In other mouse tumor models,
the depletion or suppression of Tregs, a major source of
\tt T G F \{ \beta
in
the tumor microenvironment, elicits TLS formation.14–16 This in-
dicates that, in addition to expressing the requisite pro-inflam-
matory mediators of TLS formation, some tumors lack or have
overcome local immunosuppression to form TLS.
Recently, various groups have described cancer-associated
fibroblast (CAF) subsets critical for promoting adaptive immunity
in tumors. Reflecting the fibroblastic reticular cell with lymphoid
tissue organizing (LTO) properties in lymph nodes, parallel func-
tions have been described for TLS-organizing reticular CAF
(rCAF) subsets.17–22

converge to activate these TLS-promoting CAF phenotypes in
complimentary ways.23 While TNFR1/2 is required for the initial
aggregation of the reticular network, LTBR expands and orga-
nizes the TLS.24 These pathways are chiefly responsible for the
upregulation of CCL19, CCL21, and CXCL13 in CAF, while other
groups have also reported on the pro-inflammatory effects of
type 1 and type 2 interferons in stromal-mediated T cell recruit-
ment.25–27 However, most patients with cancer do not present
with TLS in their tumors, and these CAF phenotypes are rare in
PDAC, where the trademark desmoplastic stroma is governed
predominantly by TGFβ-programmed myofibroblasts (my-
CAF).28–31 Therefore, while we are beginning to understand the
positive signals that reinforce TLS formation, little is understood
about the negative regulators that restrict this biology and how to
overcome them therapeutically. In this study, we investigated the
mechanisms of how CAF phenotypic programming regulates
TLS formation. These data indicate that in tumor microenviron-
ments without TLS, immunosuppressive stromal elements coun-
teract pro-inflammatory mediators by abrogating reticular CAF
differentiation.

In all KPC tumors, surface LTBR expression was present on

agonizing LTBR is a reproducible method to induce lymphocyte
recruitment and E-TLS formation, allowing us to assess the
mechanisms that influence TLS development and function in
heterogeneous PDAC models.
CAF phenotypes associate with TLS permissiveness in
PDAC tumors
We then investigated the response of other KPC cell lines to
αLTBR treatment. While the KPC-6 and KPCL-1 lines re-
sponded to treatment with αLTBR, the KPCL-4 and KPCL-3
lines did not generate lymphoid aggregates, nor did they
exhibit a growth delay (Figure 2A). For this reason, the
KPC-6 and KPCL-1 lines were termed ‘‘TLS-permissive’’ and
the KPCL-4 and KPCL-3 lines were termed ‘‘TLS-resistant’’ al-
lowing us to study the features of tumors receptive to TLS for-
mation in vivo. Several groups have shown that specific CAF
phenotypes can regulate the lymphocyte recruitment and or-
ganization of the reticular network in other mouse tumor
models. CAF that lack expression of fibroblast-activating
protein (FAP) and alpha smooth muscle actin (αSMA) but co-
expressing the cell adhesion molecules, ICAM-1 and
VCAM-1, mark immunofibroblasts that initiate TLS formation
in vivo.17,24 Accordingly, flow cytometry assessment of fibro-

TGFβ antagonizes the TNFα/LTBR induction of reticular
programming in fibroblasts
Previous studies show TNFR1/2 and LTBR signaling in CAF
cooperate to promote LTO functions in tumors.24 To understand
how these signaling pathways regulate fibroblast differentiation
in this manner, we isolated primary dermal fibroblasts from
mice and exposed them to various treatments in vitro. Fibro-
blasts were treated with TNFα, αLTBR, or in combination and
analyzed by flow cytometry for differentiation markers. TNFα
and αLTBR treatment alone upregulated expression of
VCAM-1, which was enhanced by the co-treatment of these li-
gands (Figure 3Ai). Expression of PDGFRα was also upregulated
in treated fibroblasts with a concomitant downregulation of
PDGFRβ (Figures 3Aii-iii) while TNFα treatment alone slightly
reduced LTBR expression on fibroblasts (Figure 3Aiv). These ob-
servations and the variable response to αLTBR agonism in vivo
(Figures 2A–2C) prompted us to identify negative regulators of
TNFR/LTBR signaling in fibroblasts. PDGFRβ has been impli-
cated as a direct target of
\tt T G F \beta
signaling,48 and a marker for
TGF
\mathrm { \Delta \ddot { \beta } }
-induced myCAF differentiation.49 Furthermore, we previ-
ously showed patients with
{ \mathsf { T L S } } ^ { + }
PDAC have downregulated
\tt T G F \beta
signaling and myCAF markers such as LRRC15.7,28 To
that end, we hypothesized that the
\tt T G F \beta
induction of myCAF
programming modulates the response to LTBR agonism. In
contrast to αLTBR treatment, TGFβ1 suppressed VCAM-1,
PDGFRα, and LTBR expression but upregulated PDGFRβ
expression (Figures 3Bi-iv). Moreover,
\tt T G F \beta 1
pre-treatment of
fibroblasts prevented αLTBR-mediated increases in VCAM-1
and PDGFRα cell surface expression (Figures 3Bi-ii). Co-culture
of naive fibroblasts with tumor-conditioned media from TLS-
permissive and resistant cell lines did not reproduce these
effects, indicating paracrine tumor cell-fibroblast crosstalk is
not responsible for CAF differentiation in these contexts
(Figure S3A). Upregulation and secretion of the lymphocyte che-
moattractants, Cxcl13 and Ccl19, required co-treatment with
both TNFα and αLTBR (Figures 3Ci-ii and 4A), and again, this
was abolished by pre-treatment with TGFβ1 at the transcriptional
(Figures 3Ci-ii) and protein level (Figures 4B and 4C). Corre-
spondingly, fibroblasts upregulated canonical myCAF markers
Acta2 and Ctgf in TGFβ1-treated groups (Figures 3Ciii-iv). Other
T cell chemoattractants, such as Cxcl9 and Ccl21a, were not
significantly induced by TNFα/αLTBR treatment (Figures S3Bi-
ii), but numerous other chemokines were and could be inhibited
by
\tt T G F \beta 1
pre-treatment (Figure S3C). Co-treatment of fibro-
blasts with TNFα and soluble LIGHT resulted in a 3-fold increase
in Cxcl13 mRNA expression, but this was markedly less than with
TNFα/αLTBR (Figure S3D), indicating that the LTBR agonist anti-
body delivers a more potent signal than endogenous LTBR li-
gands. The induction of the iCAF phenotype by IL-1α treatment
or exposure to interferons alone or in combination with αLTBR
did not induce rCAF differentiation or chemokine expression
(Figures S3Ei-iii). As tissue-specific differences in fibroblast
phenotype have been demonstrated, fibroblasts from normal
mouse pancreas were isolated and compared to dermal fibro-
blasts for baseline marker expression and responsiveness to
TGFβ1 and TNFα/αLTBR. Flow cytometry revealed that both
dermal and pancreatic fibroblasts had similar expression pro-
files, including LTBR. Moreover, treatment with TGFβ1 and
TNFα/αLTBR modulated expression of VCAM-1, PDGFRα,
PDGFRβ, and LTBR similarly to dermal fibroblasts (Figures S3F
and S3G). Further analysis of in vitro activated dermal fibroblasts
showed global changes in CD105, CD34, Ly6A, CD74, CD21/
CD35, TNFR, and HVEM between rCAF and myCAF differentia-
tion conditions (Figure S3H). Taken together, these data show
that TNFR/LTBR-mediated reticular cell programming in fibro-
blasts is abrogated by the TGF
\mathrm { \Delta \ddot { \beta } }
-induction of the myCAF
phenotype.
Lymphocyte migration to reticular fibroblasts is
suppressed by TGFβ
The differentiation of rCAF phenotypes by TNFR/LTBR and lack
of CXCL13 secretion from our KPC cancer cell lines (Figure 4A)
suggest lymphocyte recruitment to these tumors is primarily
regulated by CAF, similar to the functions of fibroblastic reticular
cells in lymph nodes.50 In support of this, the TNFα/αLTBR treat-
ment of cultured fibroblasts resulted in the secretion of CXCL13
and CCL19 that was abrogated by TGFβ1 pre-conditioning
(Figures 4B and 4C). We next conducted migration assays by
culturing naive fibroblasts in the bottom well, followed by the
addition of purified B or T cells (Figures S4A–S4E) to the upper
transwell (Figure 4D). We first assessed naive B cells for their
migratory potential to activated fibroblasts (Figure S4A). Migra-
tion of naive B cells to TNFα/αLTBR-activated fibroblasts was
significantly increased compared to untreated fibroblasts
(Figure 4E) but
\tt T G F \beta 1
pre-treatment suppressed this migration
(Figure 4E). Naive

Blockade of TGFβR1 signaling reverses the suppression
of the reticular fibroblast phenotype and enhances
tumor control
Given that
{ \mathsf { T G F } } { \mathsf { \beta } }
suppressed the recruitment of lymphocytes
to reticular CAF, we hypothesized that inhibiting
{ \mathsf { T G F } } { \mathsf { \beta } }
recep-
tor (TGFβR1) signaling would reverse this effect. Indeed, naive
fibroblasts treated with a small molecule antagonist to
TGFβR1 prior to
\tt T G F \beta 1
relieved the repression of Cxcl13
and Ccl19 in TNFα/αLTBR stimulated fibroblasts (Figures 5A
and 5B) and blocked myCAF differentiation (Figures S5Ai-ii).
Importantly, the treatment of fibroblasts alone with TGFβR1i
did not upregulate
C x c I 1 3
or Ccl19, indicating a requirement
for TNFR and LTBR signaling engagement to induce chemo-

alleviated the

abolished

6M), indicating that B cell aggregates alone are not functional

rCAF are spatially associated with TLS in human
patients with PDAC
To translate these preclinical findings to patients with PDAC,
we assessed the spatial relationships between CAF phenotype
and TLS formation in human PDAC tumors using spatial
transcriptomics. We first created a TLS-aggregate signature
from spatially deconvolved transcriptomic data and validated
this from pathologist annotations of H&E sections and multispec-
tral immunofluorescence for TLS-markers (Figure S7A). The Vis-
ium-defined TLS features showed good agreement with the
immunofluorescent identification of B cells in TLS-regions
(Figure S7B). Using a publicly available single-cell RNA-
sequencing dataset53
we deconvoluted CAF phenotypes from 6
transcriptionally and spatially distinct CAF subsets (Figure S7C)
and determined their proximity to TLS adjacent regions
(Figure 7A). Indeed, rCAFs expressing CCL19 and CCL21 were
significantly enriched in the proximal regions encapsulating TLS,
while myCAFs were more frequently distributed distally to TLS
(Figures 7B and S7D). This corresponded to a significantly lower
distance of rCAF to TLS regions and a greater distance of myCAF
to TLS regions (Figure 7C). Furthermore, we identified TLS prox-
imal rCAF subsets had higher activation of TNFR and non-canon-
ical NF-κB signaling and reduced TGFβR signaling relative to my-
CAF (Figures 7D, 7E, and S7E), supporting the opposing roles of
TNFR/LTBR signaling and TGFβR signaling in these CAF subsets.
Taken together, these data strongly indicate that NFκB-activated
rCAF are necessary for TLS formation and T cell-dependent tumor
immunity, while TGFβR-differentiated myCAF opposes rCAF dif-
ferentiation and TLS neogenesis in mouse and human PDAC
tumors.
DISCUSSION
The fibrotic stroma is a significant driver of PDAC progression.
Accordingly, understanding how to therapeutically modulate
CAF phenotypic plasticity and function is a clinical priority. How-
ever, the global depletion of CAF in mouse PDAC has demon-
strated contradictory outcomes on tumor progression.54,55

Limitations of the study
Our report possesses some limitations. Despite the treatment
response and TLS-like aggregate formation to LTBR agonists
and TGFβR1 inhibitors in our mouse models, we only observed
early-TLS aggregate formation, devoid of germinal centers. We
attribute this to the lack of strong T and B cell antigens expressed
in our tumor cell lines. Indeed, the expression of hen egg lyso-
zyme in murine lung cancer lines can induce spontaneous TLS
formation.99 Second, we did not observe a functional anti-tumor
role for B cells in our model. This is in contrast to other reports in
mice and patients, and again is potentially ascribed to the lack of
high-affinity B-cell antigens expressed in our KPC lines. Finally,
the LTBR agonist likely binds endothelial cells directly to induce
transdifferentiation into an HEV phenotype, but the investigation
into how HEV contributed to TLS formation was beyond the
scope of this study and also reported elsewhere.20,100 Future
studies will be required to dissect the role of humoral responses
in TLS-enhanced anti-tumor immunity and how HEV may
contribute to this immunobiology.

spans.content: EXPERIMENTAL MODEL AND STUDY PARTICIPANT DETAILS
Human patient samples
Formalin fixed paraffin embedded tissue sections from primary PDAC tumors were collected from treatment-naı¨ve patients under-
going surgical resection at the Ohio State University Comprehensive Cancer Center (OSUCCC) under the approved IRB protocol:
OSU2023C0226 and obtained only after informed consent was granted. Patient characteristics are described in Table S2.
Animals
All mouse studies were conducted and approved under IACUC approval at The Ohio State University: 2022A00000066 and
A L K 5 ^ { t l / t I }
mice
( T g f b r \mathcal { I } ^ { \mathrm { t m 1 . 1 K a r l / K u l } } / \mathsf { J } )
were generously provided by Dr. Kristina Young. C57BL/6J and
PDGFRα-CreERT2 mice were purchased from Jackson Labs (B6.129S-Pdgfrαtm1.1(cre/ERT2)Blh/J) and crossed to
\mathsf { A L K 5 } ^ { t l / t 1 }
mice to
generate Pdgfra-CreERT2/ALK5fl/fl mice with a tamoxifen-regulated Cre excision of the TGFβR1 gene in fibroblasts. Double trans-
genic mice were treated daily with tamoxifen (75mg/kg) or vehicle by oral gavage for 5 days followed by orthotopic tumor implant
2 days later. Tamoxifen was administered orally again at days 7-9 and αLTBR was given i.p. at days 10, 14, and 17 and mice
were sacrificed at day 18.
Tumor models
PK5/PK5L PDAC cell lines used for orthotopic tumor implantation were derived from KPC (Pdx1-Cre/LSL-KRasG12D/+/LSL-
transgenic mouse tumors as previously described.34 Five of these cell lines were termed KPC-5-9 (Pdx1-Cre/LSL-
\underline { { K } } R a s ^ { G 1 2 D } / \underline { { p } } 5 3 ^ { R 1 7 2 H / w t } )
while four were termed KPCL-1-4 (Pdx1-Cre/LSL-KRasG12D/LSL-p53R172H/wt/LSL-Luciferase-SIYRYYGL).
All cells were cultured at
3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
with
\mathsf { C O } _ { 2 }
and tested with e-Myco Mycoplasma PCR Detection kit to ensure cell lines were free
from mycoplasma contamination. KPC/KPCL tumor cells were implanted orthotopically into C57BL/6 mice (Jackson Labs). Prior
to implant, tumor cells were cultured in RPMI complete medium
sodium-pyruvate,
penicillin/streptomycin,
NEAAs,
and
5 0 ~ \mu \mathsf { M }
\beta
-mercaptoethanol) until
confluency. On the day of implant, cells were harvested, washed, and resuspended
in a 50:50 RPMI serum-free
+ 5 \mathsf { m g } / \mathsf { m l }
Cultrex (R&D Systems). Orthotopic implants were performed by laparotomy survival surgeries
where the head of the pancreas was injected with a 30-gauge insulin syringe at
5 \times 1 0 \ 3
cells/mouse in
5 0 \mu \vert
total cell suspension.
Tumors were randomized at day 7 by either a Vevo2100 ultrasound (Visual sonics) for KPC tumors or by bioluminescence utilizing the
IVIS Spectrum 2 system (Perkin Elmer) for KPCL tumors. For IVIS, mice were injected with
1 5 0 \mathrm { m g / k g }
D-luciferin (GoldBio) 10 min.
prior to exposure to determine tumor luciferase fluorescence. All tumors were subsequently monitored by ultrasound following
randomization.

Generation, culture, and treatment of primary mouse fibroblasts
Primary mouse fibroblasts were isolated from the ears of 6-8-week-old C57BL/6 mice. Briefly, mice were euthanized, ears excised,
and incubated in
4 0 ~ \mathrm { m l } ~ 7 0 \%
ethanol for 5 min. Ears were dried in petri dishes and then minced into small pieces
( < 3 \mathsf { m m } )
) in RPMI
complete medium. Minced ear tissues were placed in 2 ml cryovials containing
\sim 1 . 8 \mathsf { m }
of
0 . 2 \mu \mathsf { m }
sterile-filtered digestion mix con-
taining
2 . 5 \ : \mathrm { m g / m ! }
collagenase IV (Worthington)
+ 1 . 2 5 \mathrm { m g / m l }
pronase (Sigma). Digestion was performed on a shaker for 90 min. at
200 rpm at
3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
. Digested ear tissues were grinded with a cell pestle and filtered through a
7 0 \mu \mathrm { m }
cell strainer. Cells were washed
twice with 10 ml complete medium and centrifuged for 7 min. at 1200 rpm. Cells were resuspended and plated with
2 5 0 \mu \ g / \mathrm { m } \mu
am-
photericin B (ThermoFisher). On day 3, complete medium was replaced with amphotericin B. Primary mouse fibroblasts were
expanded and frozen at low passage
. Primary mouse fibroblasts were cultured in RPMI complete medium and used after
3 or less passages in culture to maintain primary cell status for all in vitro experiments. Primary mouse pancreatic fibroblasts
were isolated from the pancreas of 6-8-week-old C57BL/6 mice using the protocol described for dermal fibroblasts above with slight
modifications. DNase I
( 5 0 \mu \ g / \mathsf { m } \mu \mu \mu \mu \mu \mu \mu \mu \mu
– Worthington) was added to the collagenase-pronase digestion mixture and pancreatic fibroblasts
were cultured on Cultrex BME
( 0 . 1 \mathrm { m g / m l }
- R&D Systems) for all in vitro experiments. Cytokines, antibodies, and inhibitors used for
treatments as indicated included: recombinant murine LIGHT (25 ng/ml – Peprotech), recombinant murine TNFα (5 ng/ml – Pepro-
tech), recombinant murine IL1α (5 ng/ml – Peprotech), recombinant murine IFNg (5 ng/ml – Peprotech), recombinant murine
\mathsf { I F N \beta }
(5 ng/ml – Biolegend), recombinant human
\tt T G F \beta 1
(5 ng/ml – Peprotech), mouse monoclonal anti-CXCL13
1 5 \mu { \sf g } / \sf m \mu
– Invitrogen),
mouse monoclonal anti-CCR7
( 5 ~ \mu \varrho / \mathsf { m } |
– R&D Systems), TGFβR1i – LY3200882
1 0 ~ \mu \mathsf { M }
– SelleckChem), 4-hydroxytamoxifen
(4-OHT) (200 nM – MedChemExpress), and mouse monoclonal anti-LTBR
( 1 \ \mu \mathsf { g } / \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l }
– Dr. Carl Ware).
METHOD DETAILS
Treatment of tumor-bearing mice
Tumor-bearing mice were not treated until mice were randomized by tumor volume and tumors reached
{ \sim } 2 5 ~ \mathsf { m m } ^ { 2 }
which occurred
around day 7-10 unless otherwise indicated. In vivo treatments were performed as follows: anti-LTBR monoclonal antibody (Dr. Carl
Ware) i.p. 3mg/kg, TGFβR1i - LY2157299 (SelleckChem) via oral gavage at
1 5 0 \mathrm { m g / k g }
dissolved in carboxymethylcellulose sodium,
tamoxifen via oral gavage at 15 mg/kg dissolved in corn oil, TGFβR1i – SM16 (MedChemExpress) in mouse chow at
0 . 3 \mathsf { g } / \mathsf { k g }
(Tocris
Biosciences), gemcitabine (i.v. 15 mg/kg) and nab-paclitaxel (i.p. 33 mg/kg) dual chemotherapy, anti-CXCL13 neutralizing antibody
(Vaccinex) i.p.
mouse, anti-CD4 depletion antibody (BioXCell) i.p.
mouse, anti-
\mathtt { C D 8 \beta }
(Lyt 3.2) depletion antibody
(BioXCell) i.p. 100 μg/mouse, anti-CD20 depletion antibody (BioXCell) i.p.
mouse, and rat IgG2a isotype control antibody
(BioXCell) i.p.
mouse. Both male and female mice were used according to the sex of the KPC cell line reported in the resource
table. Mice 6-12 weeks of age at the time of tumor induction were used for all experiments.
Flow cytometry
Orthotopic tumors were removed and half of the tumor with spleen attached was saved for histology (see Immunohistochemistry).
Remaining tumor portion was weighed and collected into 1 ml of DMEM serum-free media and placed on ice. Tumors were minced
into small pieces, transferred to stir bottles containing
1 5 \mathsf { m } |
digestion media (1 mg/ml collagenase IV (Worthington)
+ 5 0 \mathsf { U } / \mathsf { m } \mathsf { l }
DNase I
(Worthington)), and digested on a stirring platform for 35 min. at 400 rpm at
3 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }
. Following digestion, cells and remaining tissue
chunks were gently grinded and filtered through a
1 0 0 ~ { \mu \mathrm { m } }
cell strainer. Cells were washed by centrifugation for 5 min. at
1300 rpm and resuspended in FACS buffer prior to downstream flow cytometry staining or freezing. Flow cytometry data was
analyzed by FlowJo software (v10.10.0) and advanced clustering analysis was performed using OMIQ. The optimal number of clus-
ters was determined using elbow metaclustering with 10 iterations and a Euclidean distance metric.

spans.content: CAF sorting from PDAC tumors
For sorting of CAFs from KPC or KPCL tumors, single-cell tumor suspensions were enriched for PDPN+ CAF by dead cell removal
(EasySep mouse dead cell removal kit, StemCell) and
\mathtt { C D 4 5 ^ { + } }
cell depletion (EasySep mouse CD45 positive selection kit, StemCell).
Cells were then stained for 30 minutes with anti-mouse CD45-BV510 (30-F11; BD), CD31-BUV496 (MEC13.3; BD), and PDPN-
PerCP/EF710 (8.1.1; ThermoFisher). Immediately prior to sorting,
5 ~ { \mu \mu }
of propidium iodide (Invitrogen) was added to the sample,
mixed well, and incubated for 5 min. prior to acquisition of PI- CD45- CD31- PDPN+ CAF on an Aurora CS (Cytek).
RT-PCR
RNA lysates were collected from primary fibroblast cultures after 24 hr. treatments by adding Buffer RLT (Qiagen) to lyse the cells.
Prior to RNA isolation, lysates were homogenized using QIAshredder columns (Qiagen) and RNA isolation was carried out using the
Qiagen RNeasy Micro RNA isolation kit (Qiagen) according to manufacturer’s instructions. Purified RNA was reverse transcribed into
cDNA using the High-Capacity cDNA Reverse Transcription kit (Applied Biosystems) and cDNA synthesis carried out in a T100 ther-
mal cycler (Bio-Rad). 50 ng of RNA was added to each qPCR reaction and prepared in TaqMan Fast Advanced master mix (Applied
Biosystems). qRT-PCR reaction was carried out in a QuantStudio 3 (Applied Biosystems) and RT-PCR data analyzed by QuantStudio

All qRT-PCR data is represented as ΔΔCT fold change normalized to cellular Tbp levels.
ELISA, Luminex, and chemokine array assays
Supernatants for ELISA were collected from primary fibroblast cultures and frozen at
\varnothing 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf { C }
prior to protein detection. Mouse
CXCL13/BLC (Biolegend), CCL19/MIP-3 beta (Fisher Scientific), and CXCL9 (Fisher Scientific) ELISA kits were used according to
manufacturer’s instructions to quantify protein expression. Luminex assays were performed using the ProcartaPlex Mouse immune
monitoring panel, 48-plex (ThermoFisher Scientific) as per the manufacturer’s instructions. Supernatants from KPC/KPCL cells were
collected after each cell line reached
confluency in a T-75 culture flask (2-3 days culturing). Luminex plates were read and
analyzed on a MAGPIX instrument (ThermoFisher) with absolute protein concentrations
( \mathsf { p g } / \mathsf { m l } )
determined via a standard curve.
Fibroblast chemokine array was performed with the Proteome Profiler Mouse Chemokine array kit (R&D Systems) according to
the manufacturer’s protocol. Supernatants from primary mouse fibroblasts at
confluency were collected after treatments
as described. Membranes were imaged using a ChemiDoc MP imaging system (Bio-Rad) and chemokine abundance assessed
by integrated density measurements in ImageJ.
In vitro T cell differentiation
Naı¨ve
\mathtt { C D 4 } ^ { + }
T cells were isolated from the spleens of 6–8-week-old BL/6 female mice and purified using the EasySep mouse naı¨ve
\mathtt { C D 4 } ^ { + }
T cell isolation kit (StemCell Technologies) according to manufacturer’s instructions. Purity was confirmed by flow cytometry to
be
by
\mathsf { C D } 4 5 ^ { + } \mathsf { C D } 4 ^ { + }
CD8- staining. One day prior to T cell differentiation, plates were coated with anti-CD3e (145-2C11, Bio-
legend) and anti-CD28 (37.51, Biolegend): Th1
( 5 ~ \mu \varrho / \mathsf { m } \mu
of each). Naı¨ve
\mathtt { C D 4 } ^ { + }
T cells were then polarized for 3 days according to
the following conditions: Th1 (cytokines – IL-12 (5 ng/ml - Peprotech), IL-2
2 5 0 \mathrm { U } / \mathrm { m } |
– Millipore Sigma) and neutralizing antibodies –
anti-IL-4 (
1 0 \mu \ g / \ m \mu
– 11B11, Invitrogen)). Differentiated Th1 cells were then harvested for downstream analysis of activation by flow
cytometry or in vitro migration assays.
In vitro migration assays
Primary mouse fibroblasts or KPC-6 tumor cells were cultured and treated in a monolayer as described in the results. B cells or naı¨ve
\boldsymbol { \mathsf { C D 8 } } ^ { + }
T cells were isolated from the spleens of 6–8-week-old BL/6 female mice and purified using the EasySep mouse B cell isolation
kit (StemCell Technologies) or EasySep mouse naı¨ve
\scriptstyle { \mathtt { C D 8 } } ^ { + }

instructions. Purity was confirmed by flow cytometry to be

B cells, naı¨ve
\scriptstyle { \mathtt { C D 8 } } ^ { + }
T cells, or in vitro differentiated Th1 cells were added to
3 . 0 \mu \mathrm { m }
Transwell filters (Corning) and allowed to
migrate to the lower chamber containing a fibroblast or tumor cell (naı¨ve
\scriptstyle { \mathtt { C D 8 } } ^ { + }

five independent and representative images of each well were taken using an inverted Rebel microscope (Discover Echo). The num-
ber of migrated cells in each image was quantified in ImageJ (NIH - version 1.54d) using thresholding to eliminate background debris
and size (pixels2 – B cells and naı¨ve
\boldsymbol { \mathsf { C D 8 } } ^ { + }
T cells (20-80) and Th1 cells (20-120) to account for increase in size during activation) and
circularity (0.4-1.0 for all cells) parameters to count cells of interest. Spontaneous migration without fibroblasts was subtracted from
final values.
PCR for Cre excision of ALK5
PCR for excision of the ALK5 gene in fibroblasts was carried out on genomic DNA (Qiagen DNeasy Blood & Tissue kit) from
dermal fibroblasts isolated from PDGFRα-CreERT2/ALK5fl/fl mice. PCR was carried out with Terra PCR direct polymerase mix
(Takara Bio) with a 58C annealing temperature and a three-primer system using the following primers: ALK5WT F (lnl5′ )
ATGAGTTATTAGAAGTTGTT, ALK5WT R (lnl3′ ) ACCCTCTCACTCTTCCTGAGT, and ALK5KO R (llox3′ ) GGAACTGGGAAAGGAGA-
TAAC.67 PCR products were electrophoresed on a
agarose gel stained with SYBR Safe DNA gel stain (Invitrogen).
Immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence
Standard staining procedures were utilized for IHC staining on 5-micron FFPE sections. Tissue was fixed overnight in
NBF prior
to paraffin embedding. Primary antibodies for IHC included anti-mouse αSMA (ab150301; Abcam), pSMAD2 (ab188334; Abcam),
CXCL13 (ab199043; Abcam), PNAd (120802, Biolegend) and CD8a (14-0808-82; Invitrogen). Sequential IHC for all primary antibodies
was performed with DAB using ImmPACT DAB substrate kit (#SK-4105; Vector Laboratories). Hematoxylin was used as a nuclear
counterstain. Hematoxylin and eosin staining of mouse tissue sections was performed according to standard procedures. Whole
slide scans of stained tissue sections were acquired with an Olympus VS200 digital scanner and images were analyzed in QuPath
version 0.5.1. αSMA and pSMAD2 staining was quantified as the percent area of highly stained pixels relative to total tumor area. CD8
staining was quantified as the number of positively stained cells relative to total cells detected in the tumor tissue area. For immu-
nofluorescence staining of 5-micron FFPE human sections or mouse tumor sections, standard multiplex procedures were used.
Briefly, tissues were baked at
{ \tt6 0 ^ { \circ } C }

). Slides were washed in

{ \mathfrak { g o o c } }
, then held at this tem-

Bulk RNA sequencing of mouse and human PDAC tumors
RNA isolation and sequencing of human PDAC tumors was performed as previously described.7,101 Mouse KPC or KPCL tumors
used for RNA-sequencing were implanted subcutaneously and harvested at day 21 post implant and snap frozen in liquid nitrogen.
mRNA was isolated using a RNeasy plus mini kit (Qiagen) according to manufacturer’s instructions. RNA quality was determined on a
nanodrop (ND-1000) and the quantity of RNA was determined on Qubit 4 fluorometer using the Qubit RNA HS assay kit (Invitrogen).
To prepare for sequencing, samples were processed using an Illumina TruSeq® Stranded mRNA Library Prep kit with Illumina TruSeq
RNA Single Indexes Set A and Set B barcoding kits. An Illumina NovaSeq 6000 S1 Reagent Kit v1.5 was used to make RNA libraries
along with a NovaSeq XP 2-Lane Kit v1.5. RNA libraries were sequenced on NovaSeq 6000. Demultiplexed fastq files for all samples
were first processed with FastQC for general quality control. All the samples in the sequencing run passed read level QC with at least
17 million reads per sample. Average read depth across all samples were 51 million reads. Raw Illumina BCL data was demultiplexed
using Illumina bcl2fastq2 v2.20. Gene expression counts were quantified using salmon-v.1.1.0 76 for all samples sequenced. Differ-
ential gene expression analysis was performed using the R software package edgeR77.

Gene set enrichment analysis
Gene set testing was performed for both spatial and single cell transcriptomics using the fastGSEA R package and msigdb for gene
sets. Reactome and Hallmark gene set sources were used and refined to just the sets containing ‘‘TNF’’ or ‘‘TGF’’ in their name.
Log2FC values were used as the scoring metric and gsea was run using the standard fgsea function, which performs their permu-
tation based gsea method.
Visium TLS identification and analysis
To identify probable TLS aggregates in Visium samples, spatial deconvolution was performed using the aforementioned single cell
dataset as reference. For each Visium sample, deconvolution was performed using TACCO python package, raw Visium read counts,
and the single cell reference set. Deconvolution results were exported as barcode cell proportion counts in csv format and incorpo-
rated into their respective Seurat objects. To identify spot aggregates of probable TLS, the spot proportions for all lymphocytes were
summed to a total lymphoid proportion. Spots were then selected based on a lymphoid proportion of 0.5 or greater and further
refined to only spots above the threshold and in groups of 3 direct neighbors also above the threshold, to be consistent with pathol-
ogist-defined TLS size constraints. TLS-adjacent spots were then isolated from each of these aggregates by selecting all spots within
a 300-micron border around each aggregate. For background distributions, 7 spot clusters within tissue were randomly selected, and
filtered for those non-overlapping with the TLS and TLS-adjacent annotated areas. Random selections were limited to a number
equivalent to 1/100 of the available non-annotated spots, but no sample had less than 25 sampled 7-spot clusters. Quantities of
cell types in these regions were averaged for all spots of the same annotation within each sample individually. For analyses involving
individual spot aggregates, TLS aggregates were individually clustered using the default iGraph package graph clustering on un-
weighted adjacency matrices of the TLS aggregate annotated spots.
CAF-TLS association analysis
To identify the spatial relationship between TLS and CAF subtypes, two approaches were used. First, Visium spots were annotated in
each sample as rCAF or myCAF enriched if they had greater than
abundance in the spot. To capture distance between TLS
aggregates and CAF species,
{ \mathsf { r C A F } } +
and myCAF+ spots were coupled to their nearest TLS aggregate centroid. Then a Euclidean
distance between
{ \mathsf { r C A F } } +
or myCAF+ spots and their nearest TLS-aggregate was captured. The distance for rCAF and myCAF to their
nearest aggregate was summarized as an average for that TLS aggregate cluster. Distances between TLS aggregate clusters and
CAF species, for every sample, were aggregated altogether for analysis, but showed no apparent preference for any specific sample.
The second method includes radial abundance for each TLS aggregate cluster. Briefly, each for TLS aggregate cluster in each sam-
ple, spot distances and rCAF/myCAF abundances were collected for all spots within 2mm of the cluster centroid. Spots which were
previously annotated as TLS were excluded, due to relative exclusion of all non-lymphoid species. CAF abundances were modeled
as a function of distance for all TLS aggregate clusters, in all samples, with generalized additive models (GAM), using the mgcv R
package, which was optimal due to the large quantity of spots. Each CAF type was smoothed with a
confidence interval to illu-
minate the relationship between distance and abundance, for each type. Significance testing on the GAM smoothed curves between
rCAF and myCAF was performed using approximate F-tests on the spline basis.
QUANTIFICATION AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
Statistical analysis
All statistical analyses for in vitro and in vivo experiments were performed using GraphPad Prism (v10.3.1). Statistical tests used
included unpaired t test, one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s multiple comparison test, or log rank Mantel-Cox test to evaluate Kaplan-
Meier survival curves as appropriate and indicated in figure legends. A p-value
was considered significant. For analysis of
migration assays, error bars represent standard error of the mean (SEM) where values from five visual fields were averaged to pro-
duce a single value for each well, which was averaged between three biological replicates to produce final error bars for each group.
